Definition: Total number of hours actually worked by all employed persons, including employees and self-employed. Preferred labour-input concept in OECD productivity analysis, covering full-time, part-time, part-year workers, overtime and additional jobs, while excluding non-worked hours such as holidays and leave.

Total labour compensation in current prices. In the OECD framework, labour remuneration comprises gross wages and salaries plus employers’ social contributions; when needed for total labour costs, compensation for self-employed workers is imputed using the average remuneration of employees within the relevant unit of analysis.

Gross value added at current prices, defined as gross output minus intermediate consumption. In the OECD productivity framework, GVA is a production-side output measure; at industry level it is measured at basic prices. Suitable denominator for OECD-based labour-share calculations such as labour compensation / GVA.

Growth rate of total labour compensation used in OECD productivity and unit labour cost analysis. Labour remuneration includes wages and salaries plus employers’ social contributions, with self-employed labour income approximated under the OECD methodology when constructing total labour costs.
Gross value added in volume terms (real), chain-linked to remove price effects. This is the real output concept used in OECD productivity analysis and robustness checks over time.

Gross value added in real terms, chain-linked and rebased to 2020. Appropriate for time-series comparisons of output levels or for plotting/robustness checks; not needed when analysing nominal shares.

Capital deepening defined as changes in capital intensity, where capital intensity is capital services per hour worked. This variable measures the growth rate of Kservices/H, not capital stock per hour. It is therefore the preferred OECD indicator for dynamic CES specifications in differences.

Capital services are the flow of productive services provided by capital assets and represent the conceptually appropriate measure of capital input for productivity analysis. In the OECD framework, aggregate capital-services growth is constructed from productive/net capital stocks by asset using user-cost weights.
